_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against 2019 EULAR/ACR SLE classification criteria, 2023 EULAR SLE treatment recommendations, BLISS-52/76 (belimumab), TULIP-1/2 (anifrolumab), LUNAR (rituximab in lupus nephritis), MAINTAIN/Euro-Lupus/ALMS for nephritis induction-maintenance, and Hopkins Lupus Cohort long-term outcome data.

h1Title: Lupus (SLE): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), commonly called lupus, is a chronic autoimmune disease in which the immune system loses tolerance to nuclear self-antigens and attacks tissue across multiple organ systems. Roughly 200,000 to 250,000 adults in the United States carry the diagnosis, with a striking 9-to-1 female predominance and onset typically between ages 15 and 45. Presentation varies widely — fatigue, joint pain, malar rash, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, blood-count drops, and kidney involvement are the most common features. Lupus nephritis develops in 30 to 50 percent of patients and is the single strongest predictor of long-term mortality. Modern treatment built around hydroxychloroquine plus targeted biologics has lifted 10-year survival above 90 percent in high-resource settings, but flares, organ damage, and accelerated cardiovascular disease remain real risks.

keyStats.prevalence: 53-72 per 100,000 in US adults; ~204,000 cases (Lupus Foundation/CDC 2018)
keyStats.demographics: 9:1 female-to-male ratio; 3-4x higher prevalence and severity in Black, Hispanic, and Asian populations
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset age 15-45; pediatric and late-onset (>50) forms exist and have distinct courses
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 3.4-5 million worldwide (Lupus Foundation of America)

definition: Systemic lupus erythematosus (ICD-10: M32) is a relapsing-remitting autoimmune disease driven by loss of tolerance to nucleic-acid-containing self-antigens, persistent type I interferon signaling, and immune-complex deposition that injures skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosal membranes, lungs, heart, and central nervous system. The pathophysiology centers on impaired clearance of apoptotic debris, B-cell hyperactivity, autoreactive T-helper responses, and chronic activation of plasmacytoid dendritic cells producing interferon-alpha. The 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria require a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer of at least 1:80 as the entry criterion, plus weighted clinical and immunologic criteria totaling 10 or more points across seven clinical and three immunologic domains. SLE is staged informally as mild (skin, joints, fatigue), moderate (cytopenias, serositis), or severe and organ-threatening (proliferative nephritis, neuropsychiatric disease, pulmonary hemorrhage, mesenteric vasculitis). It sits at the center of the connective-tissue disease family alongside Sjögren's, scleroderma, polymyositis, and antiphospholipid syndrome, with which it frequently overlaps. Rheumatologists lead care, with nephrology, dermatology, hematology, obstetrics, and cardiology involved depending on organ involvement.

typesClassification.description: The classic multisystem form. Defined by the 2019 EULAR/ACR criteria. Severity ranges from mild mucocutaneous and joint disease to organ-threatening nephritis and neuropsychiatric involvement.

Skin-limited disease subtypes — acute (malar rash), subacute (annular and papulosquamous photosensitive lesions, often anti-Ro positive), and chronic (discoid lupus). About 20 percent of CLE patients progress to systemic disease over a decade.

Triggered by hydralazine, procainamide, minocycline, isoniazid, anti-TNF agents, and certain checkpoint inhibitors. Anti-histone antibodies are typical; renal and CNS involvement is rare. Resolves within weeks to months of stopping the drug.

Caused by transplacental anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. Presents with photosensitive rash and, in 2 percent, congenital complete heart block. Cutaneous features resolve as maternal antibodies clear; heart block is permanent.

ISN/RPS class I-VI renal involvement, with classes III and IV (proliferative) carrying the highest risk of progression to kidney failure. Develops in 30-50 percent of SLE patients within five years of diagnosis.

primarySymptoms: Profound fatigue that is disproportionate to activity and not relieved by sleep — present in over 80 percent of patients and often the most disabling day-to-day symptom.
Symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis or arthralgia involving the small joints of the hands, wrists, and knees, typically non-erosive and worse with morning stiffness lasting under one hour.
Malar (butterfly) rash across the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, triggered or worsened by ultraviolet exposure — occurs in roughly 30-50 percent of patients.
Photosensitive skin eruptions on sun-exposed areas including the V of the neck, dorsal forearms, and upper back, often appearing 24-72 hours after sun exposure.
Painless oral or nasal ulcers, typically on the hard palate, that recur in clusters during flares.
Non-scarring or scarring alopecia, with diffuse hair thinning and so-called lupus hair — short, fragile, broken hairs along the frontal hairline.
Raynaud phenomenon — fingers turn white, then blue, then red on cold exposure — affecting 30-40 percent of patients and often preceding diagnosis by years.
Serositis presenting as pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea from pleural effusions, or pericarditis with positional retrosternal pain.
Foamy urine, ankle swelling, or hypertension signaling lupus nephritis; often clinically silent until proteinuria is detected on routine testing.
Hematologic features — easy bruising from thrombocytopenia, fatigue from autoimmune hemolytic anemia, or recurrent infections from leukopenia.
Neuropsychiatric features including cognitive dysfunction (lupus fog), mood disorders, seizures, stroke, and rarely psychosis — affecting up to 40 percent across the disease course.
Low-grade fevers without infectious cause, often heralding a flare and responding to corticosteroids within 48-72 hours.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained low-grade fever, fatigue, and arthralgia persisting more than six weeks, especially in a woman aged 15-45
Photosensitive facial rash that flares 24-72 hours after sun exposure and lasts longer than ordinary sunburn
Recurrent painless oral ulcers on the hard palate, often noticed only on dental exam
Persistent leukopenia or lymphopenia on routine bloodwork without an obvious cause
Positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) at titer 1:160 or higher, especially with a homogeneous or speckled pattern
emergencySigns: Acute confusion, seizure, focal neurological deficit, or sudden severe headache — neuropsychiatric lupus, stroke, or CNS vasculitis must be excluded urgently
Hemoptysis with dyspnea and falling hemoglobin — diffuse alveolar hemorrhage carries 50 percent mortality without prompt immunosuppression
Rapidly rising creatinine, active urinary sediment with red-cell casts, and new proteinuria — suggests rapidly progressive lupus nephritis requiring urgent biopsy
High fever, hypotension, and pancytopenia in a patient on immunosuppressants — sepsis or macrophage activation syndrome are both life-threatening
Severe abdominal pain with peritoneal signs — lupus mesenteric vasculitis or bowel ischemia warrants emergency imaging and surgical review

causes.description: Over 100 loci are associated with SLE, including HLA-DR2 and HLA-DR3, complement deficiencies (C1q, C2, C4), IRF5, STAT4, BLK, and TNFAIP3. Sibling recurrence risk is 8-29 fold, and monozygotic twin concordance reaches 25-50 percent.

Estrogens enhance B-cell survival and autoantibody production, and the X chromosome carries several immune-regulatory genes (TLR7, CD40LG, FOXP3). Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) confers SLE risk similar to female chromosomal complement, supporting an X-dose effect.

causes.cause: Epstein-Barr virus and other viral triggers
causes.description: Nearly 99 percent of SLE patients show prior EBV infection versus 70 percent of controls. Molecular mimicry between EBNA-1 and Ro/SSA, plus persistent B-cell latency, contribute to loss of tolerance in genetically susceptible hosts.
UV-B induces keratinocyte apoptosis, exposes nuclear antigens, upregulates type I interferon, and triggers cutaneous and systemic flares. UV-induced flare is reported by 60-80 percent of patients.
Doubles the risk of SLE onset and reduces hydroxychloroquine effectiveness. Smokers have more cutaneous disease, higher anti-dsDNA titers, and accelerated cardiovascular damage.
Occupational silica exposure (mining, construction, sandblasting) raises SLE risk roughly 2-4 fold through chronic innate immune activation and accelerated apoptosis.

Hydralazine, procainamide, minocycline, isoniazid, anti-TNF biologics, and immune checkpoint inhibitors can trigger a drug-induced lupus syndrome that typically remits after withdrawal. Anti-histone antibodies are the serologic hallmark.

riskFactors.description: Women account for 90 percent of adult-onset SLE; the gap is narrower in pediatric and elderly-onset disease. Sex-linked immune-gene dose and estrogen effects on B cells are the leading drivers.
Onset peaks during reproductive years. Pediatric onset accounts for 10-20 percent of cases and carries higher rates of nephritis and CNS involvement.

riskFactors.factor: African, Hispanic, Asian, or Native American ancestry
riskFactors.description: Black women have 3-4 times the prevalence of white women, earlier onset, more nephritis, and 2-3 times higher mortality. Hispanic and Asian populations show similar severity skew.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of SLE or other autoimmune disease
riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with lupus raises personal risk 8-29 fold. Family history of thyroid disease, rheumatoid arthritis, or type 1 diabetes also signals shared autoimmune predisposition.
Doubles SLE risk and worsens cutaneous disease. Smoking reduces hydroxychloroquine blood levels by roughly 30 percent and is consistently associated with flare frequency.
UV-B triggers cutaneous and systemic flares in 60-80 percent of patients. Daily broad-spectrum sunscreen and sun-protective clothing reduce flare frequency in controlled studies.
Mining, construction, foundry, and sandblasting work raises SLE risk 2-4 fold. Risk persists for decades after exposure ends.

riskFactors.factor: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives in high-risk patients
riskFactors.description: Probably small absolute risk in most patients; avoided in those with antiphospholipid antibodies or active disease where thrombotic and flare risks rise.
Common in SLE due to sun avoidance and impaired conversion. Levels below 20 ng/mL associate with higher disease activity scores and more nephritis flares.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and limited specialist access
riskFactors.description: Delayed diagnosis, inconsistent hydroxychloroquine adherence, and reduced biologic access drive worse outcomes independent of biology.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: approximately 204,000 adults (CDC SLE registries 2002-2018); 90 percent female
Black women: incidence 9.4 per 100,000 per year, prevalence ~196 per 100,000 — highest of any major US group
Hispanic women: incidence 4.7 per 100,000 per year, with earlier onset and higher nephritis rates than non-Hispanic white women
Asian and Pacific Islander women: comparable severity to Black and Hispanic women, especially in lupus nephritis
Pediatric onset (<18 years): 10-20 percent of SLE; higher rates of class III/IV nephritis (60-80 percent versus 30-50 percent in adults)
Late-onset (>50 years): 4-18 percent of SLE; more pulmonary and serosal disease, less nephritis, often milder course
diagnosisOverview: Lupus diagnosis is clinical, supported by serology and confirmed by the 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria. Workup starts with a careful multisystem history — fatigue, photosensitive rash, joint pain, oral ulcers, Raynaud, fevers, hair loss, pleuritic chest pain — and a focused examination for malar rash, oral ulcers, alopecia, synovitis, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and peripheral neuropathy. A positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) at titer 1:80 or higher is the obligatory entry criterion: a negative ANA on standard HEp-2 substrate makes SLE highly unlikely. Once ANA is positive, the 2019 criteria assign weighted points across seven clinical domains (constitutional, hematologic, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, renal) and three immunologic domains (antiphospholipid antibodies, complement, SLE-specific antibodies). A patient with at least one clinical criterion and a total weight of 10 or more is classified as having SLE. Anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies are 95 percent specific and correlate with disease activity, particularly nephritis. Anti-Smith antibodies are even more specific (99 percent) but only present in 25-30 percent. Low complement (C3, C4) and rising anti-dsDNA titers indicate active disease. Antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I) should be measured at baseline because they alter pregnancy planning and thrombosis risk. Urinalysis with microscopy is essential at every visit — proteinuria above 0.5 g/day, red-cell casts, or unexplained creatinine rise triggers a kidney biopsy to determine ISN/RPS class, which directs treatment. Skin biopsy is helpful for atypical rashes and may show interface dermatitis with positive lupus band on direct immunofluorescence. Differential diagnoses to exclude include early rheumatoid arthritis, mixed connective tissue disease, primary Sjögren's, viral infections (parvovirus B19, EBV), drug-induced lupus, and undifferentiated connective tissue disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Antinuclear antibody (ANA) by HEp-2 indirect immunofluorescence
diagnosticTests.purpose: Entry criterion for SLE classification — a titer of 1:80 or higher is required. Reports staining pattern (homogeneous, speckled, nucleolar, centromere) which guides further antibody testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single tube of blood drawn from an arm vein. Results return in 2-5 days. A positive result is non-specific — about 5-15 percent of healthy adults have a low-titer ANA, so clinical context matters.

diagnosticTests.test: Extractable nuclear antigen panel (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB, anti-RNP, anti-histone)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms SLE specificity. Anti-dsDNA correlates with nephritis activity; anti-Sm is highly specific; anti-Ro/La predict neonatal lupus risk; anti-histone signals drug-induced lupus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drawn from the same blood sample as ANA. Results return in 5-10 days. Anti-dsDNA is often repeated every 3-6 months to monitor activity.

diagnosticTests.test: Complement C3 and C4 with CH50
diagnosticTests.purpose: Low complement reflects consumption by immune complexes and signals active disease, especially nephritis. Rising complement with treatment indicates response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test, results same day in most labs. Trended over time alongside anti-dsDNA.

diagnosticTests.test: Antiphospholipid antibody panel (lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin IgG/IgM, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG/IgM)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies thrombotic and obstetric risk. Triple positivity warrants pre-pregnancy counseling and may justify primary thromboprophylaxis with low-dose aspirin or hydroxychloroquine.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; lupus anticoagulant testing requires the patient to be off direct oral anticoagulants. Confirmation requires a second positive result 12 weeks apart.

diagnosticTests.test: Urinalysis with microscopy and spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for lupus nephritis at every visit. Proteinuria above 0.5 g/day, hematuria, or red-cell casts triggers kidney biopsy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single urine sample at the clinic. Results return same day. Repeated at least every 3 months in stable patients and more often during flares.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines lupus nephritis class (I-VI), activity, and chronicity. Class III and IV (proliferative) require aggressive induction therapy; class V (membranous) is managed differently; class VI is fibrotic and irreversible.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound-guided needle biopsy under local anesthetic, usually as a day case. Overnight monitoring for bleeding is routine. Results take 5-7 days to return.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, ESR, CRP
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects cytopenias (autoimmune hemolytic anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia), renal function, and inflammation. ESR is typically elevated in active SLE; CRP is often normal — a rise suggests infection or serositis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draws. CBC repeated monthly during induction and every 3 months when stable.

diagnosticTests.test: Imaging targeted to symptoms (echocardiogram, chest X-ray or CT, MRI brain)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Echocardiogram for suspected pericarditis or Libman-Sacks endocarditis; chest imaging for pleuritis, effusions, or interstitial lung disease; MRI brain for neuropsychiatric symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Modality depends on symptom; echocardiogram and chest X-ray are painless and same-day; MRI brain is 30-45 minutes and requires lying still.

treatmentOverview: SLE treatment is built around three goals: induce remission of active disease, prevent flares and organ damage, and minimize long-term glucocorticoid exposure. The 2023 EULAR recommendations make hydroxychloroquine the foundation of care for every SLE patient regardless of severity, dosed at no more than 5 mg/kg of actual body weight per day to limit retinal toxicity. Hydroxychloroquine reduces flares by roughly 50 percent, protects against thrombosis, halves nephritis risk, and improves long-term survival. Glucocorticoids remain the most rapid-acting anti-inflammatory, but the modern approach limits maintenance prednisone to 5 mg per day or less, with rapid taper to avoid cataracts, osteoporosis, infection, and cardiovascular damage. Steroid-sparing immunosuppressants are added based on organ involvement: methotrexate or azathioprine for joint and skin disease; mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide for proliferative nephritis or severe extrarenal disease; calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, voclosporin) for class V or refractory nephritis. Biologics now have a defined role. Belimumab, a B-cell activating factor (BAFF) inhibitor, was the first biologic approved for SLE (BLISS-52 and BLISS-76 trials) and reduces flare rate and steroid use; it is now also approved for lupus nephritis based on the BLISS-LN trial. Anifrolumab, a type I interferon receptor blocker, was approved after the TULIP-1 and TULIP-2 trials and is used for moderate-to-severe non-renal SLE refractory to standard therapy. Rituximab (anti-CD20) is widely used off-label for severe refractory disease and is supported by the LUNAR trial as adjunct therapy in lupus nephritis. Voclosporin (AURORA-1) and obinutuzumab (NOBILITY) are newer additions for nephritis. Treat-to-target principles — aiming for the Lupus Low Disease Activity State (LLDAS) or remission as defined by DORIS — reduce damage accrual. Sun protection, smoking cessation, vaccination updates, vitamin D repletion, cardiovascular risk management, and pre-pregnancy counseling with antiphospholipid screening are integral to every care plan.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine (≤5 mg/kg actual body weight daily, typically 200-400 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Foundational therapy for every SLE patient. Interferes with toll-like receptor signaling and antigen presentation. Requires annual retinal screening after 5 years of use. Continued through pregnancy and breastfeeding.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces flare rate by roughly 50 percent, halves lupus nephritis incidence, lowers thrombotic events by ~30 percent, and improves 10-year survival in cohort studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Glucocorticoids (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg for severe disease; methylprednisolone pulse 250-1000 mg IV for organ-threatening flares)
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid-onset anti-inflammatory used for induction of severe disease and bridging until steroid-sparing agents take effect. Target maintenance dose 5 mg/day or less per 2023 EULAR; long-term high doses drive damage accrual.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in over 80 percent within 1-2 weeks; cumulative dose strongly predicts damage index (SLICC-DI) scores at 10 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (1-3 g/day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line induction and maintenance for class III/IV lupus nephritis (ALMS, MAINTAIN trials), and effective for severe extrarenal SLE. Teratogenic — switch to azathioprine before pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or partial renal response in 56-72 percent at 6 months for proliferative nephritis; non-inferior to IV cyclophosphamide with fewer side effects.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus regimen: 500 mg IV every 2 weeks × 6 doses; or NIH regimen: monthly higher dose)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe organ-threatening disease — proliferative nephritis, CNS lupus, pulmonary hemorrhage, mesenteric vasculitis. Low-dose Euro-Lupus is preferred in white European patients; outcomes by ancestry vary.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete renal response 30-40 percent at 6 months, similar to mycophenolate; long-term renal survival 10-year >80 percent in responders.

medicalTreatments.name: Belimumab (10 mg/kg IV monthly or 200 mg SC weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Human monoclonal antibody against B-cell activating factor (BAFF/BLyS). First biologic approved for SLE (BLISS-52, BLISS-76). Approved for active lupus nephritis (BLISS-LN). Onset of effect over 3-6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SRI-4 response 43-58 percent vs 34-44 percent placebo at 52 weeks; renal response in 43 percent in BLISS-LN. Reduces flares and steroid burden in extension data through 7+ years.

medicalTreatments.name: Anifrolumab (300 mg IV every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Type I interferon receptor antibody approved for moderate-to-severe non-renal SLE (TULIP-1, TULIP-2). Used in patients failing antimalarials and immunosuppressants. Avoid in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: BICLA response 47-48 percent vs 31-32 percent placebo at 52 weeks; allows steroid reduction in roughly 50 percent of responders.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (1 g IV days 0 and 14, then every 6 months as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 B-cell depleter used off-label for refractory SLE — severe cytopenias, neuropsychiatric disease, and as adjunct in lupus nephritis (LUNAR trial). Increasingly combined with belimumab in trial settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in 60-80 percent of refractory cases in observational cohorts; LUNAR did not meet its primary endpoint but real-world use remains common.

medicalTreatments.name: Voclosporin (23.7 mg twice daily) and tacrolimus
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitors that reduce proteinuria through podocyte stabilization and immune suppression. Voclosporin (AURORA-1, AURORA-2) is approved for active lupus nephritis as add-on to mycophenolate and steroids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete renal response 41 percent vs 23 percent with mycophenolate plus steroids alone at 52 weeks (AURORA-1); rapid reduction in proteinuria.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine (1.5-2.5 mg/kg/day) and methotrexate (10-25 mg weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing agents for mild-to-moderate SLE — skin, joints, serositis. Azathioprine is preferred in pregnancy planning. TPMT genotyping recommended before starting azathioprine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Allow prednisone reduction to ≤5 mg/day in roughly 60-70 percent of patients with non-organ-threatening disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for end-stage lupus nephritis. Most centers wait until SLE is serologically quiescent for at least 3-6 months before listing. Recurrence of lupus nephritis in the allograft is uncommon (2-10 percent).
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year graft survival roughly 80 percent — comparable to non-lupus transplant recipients in matched cohorts.

surgicalOptions.name: Joint replacement for avascular necrosis
surgicalOptions.description: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head from cumulative steroid exposure affects up to 10 percent of long-standing SLE. Total hip arthroplasty is the standard intervention.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief and functional improvement in over 90 percent at 5 years, comparable to non-SLE arthroplasty when infection risk is managed.

surgicalOptions.name: Pericardial window or pericardiocentesis
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for large symptomatic pericardial effusions or tamponade unresponsive to medical therapy. Most lupus pericarditis resolves with steroids alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin D repletion (1000-2000 IU daily, higher if deficient)
alternativeTreatments.description: Many SLE patients are deficient due to sun avoidance. Repletion to 30-50 ng/mL associates with lower disease activity scores in observational and small interventional studies (Petri 2013).

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation (1-3 g EPA+DHA daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Small randomized trials suggest reduced disease activity and cardiovascular risk markers. Reasonable adjunct given low risk profile and cardiovascular comorbidity in SLE.

alternativeTreatments.name: DHEA (200 mg daily, women only, specialist supervision)
alternativeTreatments.description: Modest benefit on mild disease activity and steroid reduction in randomized trials (Petri 2002, Chang 2002); androgenic side effects limit use.

whySeeSpecialist: A rheumatologist should manage every patient with suspected or confirmed SLE because the disease requires longitudinal serologic monitoring, individualized immunosuppression, and recognition of subtle flares before organ damage occurs. Patients with proliferative nephritis additionally need a nephrologist; severe cutaneous disease benefits from dermatology; pregnancy planning needs maternal-fetal medicine coordination. Primary care alone is insufficient even for mild disease because hydroxychloroquine dosing, retinal screening, antiphospholipid stratification, and vaccination strategy all require rheumatology input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a rheumatologist with active SLE caseload — ideally 50 or more patients seen yearly — and access to a multidisciplinary clinic including nephrology, dermatology, and maternal-fetal medicine. Ask about familiarity with the 2023 EULAR treatment recommendations, treat-to-target strategy, biologic prescribing (belimumab, anifrolumab, rituximab), and pregnancy management. Continuity matters: SLE is a lifelong disease, and the same clinician tracking trends over years detects flares earliest.
hospitalCriteria: Tertiary academic center with dedicated lupus or connective tissue disease clinic
On-site nephrology with renal biopsy and dialysis capability
Pharmacy access to all biologics (belimumab, anifrolumab, rituximab, voclosporin) without prior-authorization delays
Maternal-fetal medicine for high-risk pregnancy management
Ophthalmology with hydroxychloroquine retinal screening (SD-OCT and visual field testing)
Bone health and osteoporosis service given long-term steroid exposure
keyFacilities: Tertiary academic lupus centers (Hopkins Lupus Center, NYU Lupus Center, UCSF Lupus Clinic in the US)
Nephrology with biopsy and dialysis support
Specialty infusion centers for belimumab, anifrolumab, and rituximab
Multidisciplinary high-risk pregnancy clinics for SLE and antiphospholipid syndrome
preventionStrategies: Avoid ultraviolet exposure with broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen daily, UV-protective clothing, and wide-brimmed hats — UV protection reduces both cutaneous and systemic flares
Stop smoking — doubles SLE risk in healthy people and accelerates damage and reduces hydroxychloroquine effectiveness in established disease
Maintain hydroxychloroquine adherence lifelong — discontinuation increases flare risk roughly 2-3 fold within 12 months
Vaccinate against influenza, pneumococcus, herpes zoster, COVID-19, and HPV before initiating biologics; live vaccines should be avoided once on immunosuppressants
Manage cardiovascular risk aggressively — control blood pressure to under 130/80, treat dyslipidemia, and screen for accelerated atherosclerosis given 2-10 fold elevated MI risk
Plan pregnancies during 6+ months of disease quiescence, on pregnancy-compatible medications, and with antiphospholipid screening completed
lifestyleModifications: Establish a sleep schedule of 7-9 hours nightly — fatigue improves measurably with consistent sleep and pacing strategies
Engage in regular low-impact aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming) for at least 150 minutes weekly — improves fatigue, mood, and cardiovascular health without triggering flares
Adopt a Mediterranean-pattern diet rich in olive oil, fish, vegetables, and whole grains — associated with lower disease activity and cardiovascular risk
Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen every morning regardless of weather and reapply every 2 hours when outdoors
Wear UV-protective sunglasses and consider window film for car and home if photosensitivity is severe
Manage stress through mindfulness, cognitive behavioral therapy, or peer support — emotional stress is a self-reported flare trigger in over 60 percent of patients
Track symptoms, medications, and lab values in a journal or app — patterns help identify personal flare triggers

recommended: Oily fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) 2-3 times per week for omega-3 intake
Olive oil as primary fat source
Leafy greens, berries, and a wide range of vegetables for antioxidants
Whole grains, legumes, and nuts for fiber and plant protein
Adequate calcium and vitamin D — fortified dairy or supplements to offset steroid bone loss
Water — at least 2 liters daily unless restricted for kidney disease
avoid: Alfalfa sprouts and alfalfa supplements — case reports of disease activation due to L-canavanine content
Excess sodium, especially with steroid use or hypertension — under 2 g/day target
Heavy alcohol use — interferes with methotrexate and azathioprine, raises infection risk
Grapefruit juice when on cyclosporine, tacrolimus, or voclosporin — disrupts drug levels
Highly processed and sugar-sweetened foods that worsen cardiovascular and metabolic risk
Raw or undercooked meat, unpasteurized dairy, and runny eggs when on immunosuppressants — listeria and salmonella risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular low-to-moderate intensity aerobic exercise is safe and beneficial for most SLE patients. Aim for 150 minutes weekly of walking, cycling, swimming, or yoga, with strength training twice weekly. Pace activity during fatigue flares and rest joints during active arthritis. Avoid outdoor exercise during peak UV hours (10 a.m. to 4 p.m.); swim with UV-protective swimwear when possible.
dailyManagement: Take hydroxychloroquine at the same time daily — adherence is the single strongest predictor of flare-free years
Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen every morning, with reapplication every 2 hours when outdoors
Track fatigue, joint pain, rashes, and any new symptoms in a simple journal — patterns guide pre-emptive treatment changes
Attend lab monitoring (CBC, creatinine, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement) every 3 months when stable and more frequently during flares
Schedule annual retinal screening with SD-OCT after 5 years on hydroxychloroquine
Carry a medication list, allergy record, and lupus-specific emergency information when traveling
prognosis: 10-year survival in SLE has risen from under 50 percent in the 1950s to over 90 percent in high-resource settings today. Survival drops sharply for patients with lupus nephritis (10-year survival 83-88 percent overall, lower in proliferative classes with poor renal response), neuropsychiatric involvement, and antiphospholipid syndrome. Cardiovascular disease has become the leading long-term cause of death — SLE patients aged 35-44 have a 50-fold higher risk of myocardial infarction than matched controls, driven by chronic inflammation, steroid exposure, and accelerated atherosclerosis. Damage index (SLICC-DI) accrues steadily, with about 50 percent of patients showing some organ damage by 5 years and 75 percent by 10 years. The strongest modifiable predictors of good outcome are early diagnosis, sustained hydroxychloroquine use, treat-to-target achievement of Lupus Low Disease Activity State (LLDAS) or remission, low cumulative steroid exposure, and aggressive cardiovascular risk management. Hopkins Lupus Cohort and other long-term registries consistently identify Black ancestry, low socioeconomic status, late diagnosis, and high anti-dsDNA titers as risk factors for worse outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: There is no recovery to a pre-disease state — SLE is a chronic relapsing condition managed across decades. Response milestones: cutaneous and joint symptoms typically improve within 2-12 weeks of starting hydroxychloroquine plus low-dose steroids; serologic improvement (rising complement, falling anti-dsDNA) lags clinical response by 1-3 months; renal response after starting induction therapy for proliferative nephritis is assessed at 3 and 6 months, with complete response (proteinuria <0.5 g/day, stable creatinine) often taking 6-12 months. Sustained remission or LLDAS is the realistic goal; many patients cycle between low-activity and flare states throughout life. Pregnancy planning is recommended after at least 6 months of disease quiescence on compatible medications.
complications: Lupus nephritis progressing to end-stage kidney disease in 10-30 percent of class III/IV patients over 10 years — requires dialysis or transplant
Accelerated cardiovascular disease with 2-10 fold elevated myocardial infarction risk — leading long-term cause of death in SLE
Antiphospholipid syndrome with deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, and recurrent pregnancy loss — present in 30-40 percent at some point
Avascular necrosis of femoral head from cumulative steroid exposure — affects up to 10 percent of long-standing patients
Infection from immunosuppression, including opportunistic infections (Pneumocystis, herpes zoster, tuberculosis reactivation) — a major cause of mortality alongside cardiovascular events
Osteoporosis and fragility fractures from steroid use and inflammatory bone loss — DEXA screening every 1-2 years on chronic steroids
Hydroxychloroquine retinal toxicity after 5+ years of use, especially with doses above 5 mg/kg or CKD — preventable with annual SD-OCT screening
Neuropsychiatric lupus including cognitive dysfunction, stroke, seizures, psychosis — affects up to 40 percent across the disease course

supportResources.name: Lupus Foundation of America
supportResources.description: Largest US patient organization for SLE — educational resources, support groups, advocacy, and research updates.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Lupus Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that develops SLE management guidelines.

supportResources.name: Lupus Research Alliance
supportResources.description: Largest non-government funder of lupus research; clinical trial directory and patient resources.

supportResources.name: Lupus UK
supportResources.description: UK national charity providing support, regional groups, and educational materials for patients and families.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause symmetric polyarthritis, but rheumatoid arthritis produces erosive joint damage, positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies, and characteristic radiographic erosions, while lupus arthritis is non-erosive and shows ANA, anti-dsDNA, low complement, plus multisystem features (rash, cytopenias, nephritis) that rheumatoid arthritis lacks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread pain, fatigue, and cognitive complaints that overlap with lupus, but lacks objective inflammation, autoantibodies, organ involvement, or rash. ANA is negative or only weakly positive in fibromyalgia, and inflammatory markers are normal. Many lupus patients also have secondary fibromyalgia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sjögren Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sjögren's predominantly causes dry eyes and dry mouth from lacrimal and salivary gland infiltration, with anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. Lupus shares anti-Ro/La in some patients but adds nephritis, cytopenias, malar rash, and serositis. The two conditions overlap in roughly 15-20 percent of SLE patients (secondary Sjögren's).

confusedWithConditions.name: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease (MCTD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MCTD has overlapping features of lupus, scleroderma, and polymyositis with characteristically high-titer anti-U1-RNP and absent anti-dsDNA. Renal disease is uncommon in MCTD, and pulmonary hypertension is the dominant late complication, whereas lupus more often causes nephritis and accelerated atherosclerosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dermatomyositis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dermatomyositis produces proximal muscle weakness with elevated creatine kinase, plus Gottron papules and heliotrope rash. Lupus rashes are photosensitive and malar in distribution; lupus rarely causes significant myositis without overlap features. Myositis-specific antibodies separate the two.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Develops in 30-50 percent of SLE patients and is the strongest predictor of long-term mortality and damage accrual.

relatedConditions.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Antiphospholipid antibodies are present in 30-40 percent of SLE patients and drive thrombotic and pregnancy complications independent of disease activity.

relatedConditions.name: Sjögren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary Sjögren's overlaps in 15-20 percent of SLE patients, sharing anti-Ro/SSA antibodies and adding sicca symptoms to the lupus phenotype.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are autoimmune polyarthritides; rhupus describes the rare overlap with erosive arthritis, ANA, anti-dsDNA, and rheumatoid factor positivity.

relatedConditions.name: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares Raynaud phenomenon and overlap connective tissue features; both are managed by rheumatology and have overlapping autoantibody profiles.

relatedConditions.name: Discoid Lupus Erythematosus
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic cutaneous lupus subtype; 5-20 percent of patients with discoid lesions progress to systemic SLE over a decade.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease, Stage 3
relatedConditions.relevance: Common downstream consequence of lupus nephritis; influences medication choices, dosing, and cardiovascular risk.

faqs.question: What is lupus?
faqs.answer: Lupus, or systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), is a chronic autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks healthy tissue across multiple organs. It can affect skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, lungs, heart, and the nervous system. About 200,000 US adults have lupus, with women aged 15-45 most commonly affected. Modern treatment built around hydroxychloroquine, immunosuppressants, and biologics now produces over 90 percent 10-year survival.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of lupus?
faqs.answer: The earliest signs are typically unexplained fatigue, joint pain in the hands and wrists, a photosensitive facial rash, recurrent mouth ulcers, hair thinning, and Raynaud phenomenon. Many patients develop these features months to years before formal diagnosis. A positive ANA blood test in this context strongly suggests evaluation by a rheumatologist.

faqs.question: How is lupus diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Lupus is diagnosed clinically and confirmed using the 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria. Diagnosis requires a positive ANA test at titer 1:80 or higher, plus a weighted score of 10 or more across clinical and immunologic criteria. Additional tests include anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, complement levels (C3, C4), urinalysis, and sometimes a kidney biopsy.

faqs.question: Is lupus curable?
faqs.answer: Lupus is not curable, but it is highly treatable. Most patients achieve long periods of low disease activity or remission with hydroxychloroquine plus other immunosuppressants. Modern care has lifted 10-year survival above 90 percent. Treatment is lifelong, similar to managing high blood pressure or diabetes.

faqs.question: What is the butterfly rash in lupus?
faqs.answer: The butterfly (malar) rash is a flat or slightly raised red rash across both cheeks and the bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. It often worsens after sun exposure and lasts days to weeks. It appears in 30-50 percent of lupus patients and is one of the most recognizable features.

faqs.question: Can men get lupus?
faqs.answer: Yes. Men account for about 10 percent of adult lupus cases. Male SLE often presents at slightly older ages, with higher rates of nephritis, serositis, and cardiovascular complications. Diagnosis can be delayed because clinicians associate lupus primarily with women, so unexplained fatigue, joint pain, and rashes in men still warrant ANA testing.

faqs.question: Does lupus affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Lupus can complicate pregnancy with higher rates of miscarriage, preeclampsia, preterm birth, and growth restriction, especially in patients with active disease, antiphospholipid antibodies, or anti-Ro/La. Outcomes are best when pregnancy is planned after 6 months of quiescence, on pregnancy-compatible medications like hydroxychloroquine and azathioprine.

faqs.question: What is lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Lupus nephritis is kidney inflammation caused by immune-complex deposition in glomeruli. It develops in 30-50 percent of SLE patients, often silently, and is detected through proteinuria, red-cell casts, or rising creatinine. Kidney biopsy classifies it into ISN/RPS classes I-VI; classes III and IV require aggressive immunosuppression to prevent kidney failure.

faqs.question: How serious is lupus?
faqs.answer: Lupus severity ranges from mild skin and joint symptoms to organ-threatening kidney, brain, lung, or blood-vessel involvement. 10-year survival is over 90 percent with modern treatment but drops with nephritis, neuropsychiatric disease, or antiphospholipid syndrome. Cardiovascular disease is now the leading long-term cause of death.

faqs.question: What triggers a lupus flare?
faqs.answer: Common flare triggers include ultraviolet light exposure, infection, emotional stress, sleep deprivation, certain medications (sulfa antibiotics, anti-TNF drugs), discontinuation of hydroxychloroquine, surgery, and pregnancy or postpartum hormonal shifts. Identifying personal triggers through a symptom journal helps prevent recurrent flares.

faqs.question: Can lupus go into remission?
faqs.answer: Yes. Complete clinical remission (no symptoms, normal labs, off all therapy except hydroxychloroquine) is achievable in 10-20 percent of patients over time. More commonly, patients reach Lupus Low Disease Activity State (LLDAS) — minimal symptoms on stable low-dose therapy — which is associated with reduced damage and better outcomes.

faqs.question: Is lupus hereditary?
faqs.answer: Lupus is not directly inherited but has a strong genetic component. A first-degree relative with SLE raises personal risk 8-29 fold, and identical twins show 25-50 percent concordance. Over 100 susceptibility genes have been identified, including HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3, IRF5, and complement deficiencies. Environment and hormones determine which carriers develop disease.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with lupus?
faqs.answer: Avoid alfalfa sprouts and alfalfa supplements due to L-canavanine, which has been linked to disease activation. Limit excess sodium when on steroids or with hypertension, and avoid grapefruit juice when taking cyclosporine, tacrolimus, or voclosporin. Avoid raw or undercooked meats and unpasteurized dairy while on immunosuppressants.

faqs.question: How does hydroxychloroquine work in lupus?
faqs.answer: Hydroxychloroquine blocks toll-like receptor signaling in immune cells and reduces antigen presentation, lowering interferon-driven inflammation. It cuts flare rate by roughly 50 percent, halves nephritis incidence, reduces thrombotic events, and improves long-term survival. It remains the foundation of lupus therapy for nearly every patient.

faqs.question: What is the difference between lupus and rheumatoid arthritis?
faqs.answer: Both cause symmetric polyarthritis, but rheumatoid arthritis is erosive with positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies, while lupus arthritis is non-erosive and accompanied by ANA, anti-dsDNA, low complement, rash, cytopenias, and potential kidney involvement. The blood-test pattern usually distinguishes them.

faqs.question: Can sun exposure cause a lupus flare?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ultraviolet light triggers cutaneous and systemic flares in 60-80 percent of lupus patients by inducing keratinocyte apoptosis and increasing interferon production. Daily broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen, UV-protective clothing, and avoiding peak UV hours measurably reduce flare frequency.

faqs.question: What is the role of belimumab in lupus?
faqs.answer: Belimumab is a monoclonal antibody against B-cell activating factor (BAFF). It was the first biologic approved for SLE, based on the BLISS-52 and BLISS-76 trials, and is now also approved for active lupus nephritis. It reduces flares and allows steroid reduction. Typical dosing is 10 mg/kg IV monthly or 200 mg subcutaneous weekly.

faqs.question: What is anifrolumab and who should consider it?
faqs.answer: Anifrolumab is a monoclonal antibody against the type I interferon receptor, approved for moderate-to-severe non-renal SLE based on the TULIP-1 and TULIP-2 trials. It is used in patients with persistent skin, joint, or serosal disease despite standard therapy. Dosing is 300 mg IV every 4 weeks; pregnancy should be avoided during treatment.

faqs.question: How often should I see my rheumatologist?
faqs.answer: Stable lupus is typically reviewed every 3 months with labs (CBC, creatinine, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement). During active disease, biologic initiation, or pregnancy, visits are more frequent. Annual retinal screening with SD-OCT begins after 5 years on hydroxychloroquine, and DEXA scans are repeated every 1-2 years on chronic steroids.

faqs.question: Can children get lupus?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric SLE accounts for 10-20 percent of all lupus cases and tends to be more severe, with higher rates of class III/IV lupus nephritis and CNS involvement. Onset before age 5 is rare and suggests monogenic interferonopathies. Pediatric rheumatology, often co-managing with nephrology and dermatology, leads care.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outlook with lupus?
faqs.answer: Survival in lupus has improved dramatically — 10-year survival is now over 90 percent in well-resourced settings, compared with under 50 percent in the 1950s. Cardiovascular disease has become the leading long-term cause of death. Sustained hydroxychloroquine use, low cumulative steroid dose, and reaching low disease activity are the strongest predictors of good outcomes.

regionalNames.name: 全身性エリテマトーデス

metaDescription: Lupus (SLE): autoimmune disease causing fatigue, rash, joint pain, and organ damage. Symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis reviewed by our board.
